articles.title: Nancy Guthrie's neighbors report disrespectful behavior as well-wishers leave flowers and ribbons
articles.description: Neighbors of NBC "Today" show host Savannah Guthrie's missing mother are reporting disrespectful behavior near her Arizona home as interest in her disappearance grows, according to news outlets.

articles.title: Flossing Is A Pain In The Ass, But It's Crucial — These Products Make It Easier
articles.description: Say goodbye to slippery string floss (and sticking your fingers in your mouth).

articles.title: CDC boosts staff working on containing hantavirus outbreak from 3 to 100 as agency seeks to avoid Covid measures: report
articles.description: Some infectious disease experts said the CDC did not respond to the outbreak as quickly as it typically would

articles.title: Scientists udentify methylphenidate as an effective tool for combating chronic cancer related fatigue
articles.description: A new meta-analysis in the May 2026 issue of JNCCN-Journal of the National Comprehensive Cancer Network provides updated evidence that methylphenidate-type psychostimulants-a class of medication that increases dopamine and norepinephrine availability in the brain-can provide meaningful relief for cancer-related fatigue.

articles.title: Inside day two of the first international trip for the Princess of Wales since her cancer treatment
articles.description: Catherine, the Princess of Wales, is in Italy for her first international trip since being treated for cancer. CNN News Central's Sara Sidner speaks with CNN Royal Correspondent Max Foster about her focus during this visit.

articles.title: My little boy said ‘mama’ once then never spoke again... we had no clue his EYEBROWS were a sign of cruel dementia
articles.description: THE day Georgia Nona’s son, now eight, was diagnosed with childhood dementia was the “worst day of my life”, she says. Little Cody Carroll was a healthy toddler reaching milestones. But one day, he’d start regressing, slowly losing the abilities he’d learned in his short life so far. Georgia, 29, is constantly reminded that her...

articles.title: Man Quarantining After Hantavirus Outbreak Speaks Out: ‘I Was In A Really Dark Place’
articles.description: Jake Rosmarin said he’s “trying not to think about” whether he’s been infected with hantavirus as he hunkers down for six weeks in a medical facility.

articles.title: 'My 32HH boobs are so big I can't sleep or play with son - but NHS refuses reduction'
articles.description: Charlotte Innes, from Bristol, claims the NHS has refused her breast reduction request five times since September 2025 despite her 32HH breasts causing severe back pain

articles.title: How Disease Names Change—and Why PCOS Is the Latest Example
articles.description: A historian of medicine told Newsweek: "Disease names are always provisional because they are human constructs."

articles.title: Clark Woman Donates Kidney To Mother, Helping Save Nurse’s Life
articles.description: Six months after a successful transplant, the Prestinari family is sharing their story to encourage organ donor registration.

articles.title: What Are Your Evening Rituals? We Want to Know.
articles.description: Do you meditate? Play Wordle? Wear soft pajamas and sip chamomile tea? We’d like to hear about the little routines that help you wind down.

articles.title: Dyadic outlines cash runway into Q2 2027 while targeting mid-2026 alpha-lactalbumin sampling
articles.description: Dyadic International (DYAI) Q1 2026 earnings call recap: 182% revenue growth, partner launches, IBT distribution, and cash runway into Q2 2027—read more.

articles.title: American doctor who helped patients on hantavirus-hit cruise ship cleared to leave isolation
articles.description: American doctor Stephen Kornfeld, who helped care for sick passengers during the hantavirus cruise ship outbreak, was the only American sent to Nebraska’s high-security biocontainment unit after an inconclusive test result.

articles.title: PCOS is now PMOS: common misconceptions about the condition explained – video
articles.description: After more than a decade of global consultation, polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) – a condition that affects one in eight women – has a new name. The hormonal disorder, estimated to affect 170 million women worldwide, will now be known as polyendocrine metabolic ovarian syndrome (PMOS). The new name was spearheaded by the endocrinologist Prof Helena Teede, the director of Melbourne’s Monash Centre for Health Research and Implementation. For too long, experts say, the misleading nature of the term 'polycystic' in PCOS contributed to delayed diagnosis and inadequate medical care. Guardian Australia’s Matilda Boseley breaks down the common misconceptions about PMOS ‘Unprecedented’ global effort gives new name to polycystic ovary syndrome – and new hope to millions of women What is PCOS, what are the symptoms and treatment, and why is it being renamed PMOS? ‘I still want to scream’: the loneliness and confusion of living with PMOS Continue reading...

articles.title: Weight loss drug use has skyrocketed. Has it changed the way Miami dines out?
articles.description: Chef Cindy Hutson is launching a new menu at Cerveceria La Tropical brewery in Wynwood, highlighting "healthy" menu items and smaller portions.

articles.title: STAT+: Treatment for alcohol addiction is undergoing a seismic shift. Many say it’s overdue
articles.description: The surging popularity of alternatives to Alcoholics Anonymous represents a seismic shift in the alcohol treatment landscape. Many say it's overdue.

articles.title: 5 early signs of SMA for parents to look out for
articles.description: Key signs of spinal muscular atrophy include severe muscle weakness, difficulty breathing or swallowing, and missing developmental milestones.

articles.title: UK borrowing costs reflect leadership uncertainty
articles.description: James Turner, head of Global Fixed Income EMEA at BlackRock, discusses political uncertainty in the U.K. and its impact on borrowing costs.

articles.title: UK healthcare company plunges into administration - in business since 2023
articles.description: The company is a family-run, independent business.

articles.title: The man who 'died' with sepsis and came back to life
articles.description: The life-threatening reaction to infection caused Marshall Wylie to lose his legs.

articles.title: Trump-Xi meeting seen as positive signal, but market expectations are low: Analyst
articles.description: Dong Chen of Bank J Safra Sarasin, says he expects the U.S.-China trade truce to be extended as a result of the summit between U.S. President Donald Trump and Chinese President Xi Jinping. He also thinks the talks will go beyond trade and economic issues, and that China will send a clear indication that it can help in the Iran conflict resolution.

articles.title: One surprising thing can block weight loss and trigger food cravings
articles.description: A study presented at Europe's largest obesity conference has revealed a common cause of for over-eating.

articles.title: 5 foods that are upgraded with black pepper – and become healthier
articles.description: Black pepper is not only an excellent seasoning choice, but also a health powerhouse that can improve health markers and prevent diseases.

articles.title: Who are the Japanese? Huge DNA discovery rewrites history
articles.description: Scientists analyzing the genomes of thousands of people across Japan discovered evidence for a previously overlooked third ancestral group, challenging the long-accepted “dual origins” theory. The newly identified ancestry appears linked to the ancient Emishi people of northeastern Japan. Researchers also uncovered inherited Neanderthal and Denisovan DNA connected to conditions like diabetes, heart disease, and cancer.

articles.title: Eating vegetables in pregnancy may influence children’s tastes later, study finds
articles.description: A new international study suggests children may respond more positively to flavours they were exposed to during pregnancy, which could help parents trying to encourage healthy eating.

articles.title: Rise in sickle cell disease prompts NHS call for more Black blood donors
articles.description: Exclusive: Demand for blood to treat the rare disorder has soared by 132% in 10 years Demand for blood needed to treat rare disorders such as sickle cell has soared by more than 130% in 10 years, forcing the NHS to ask for more donors to come forward. Requests for haemoglobin S (HbS)-negative blood, the type most used in blood transfusions for sickle cell anaemia patients, stood at 82,181 units in 2015. But last year, more than 191,000 units were needed, a 132% increase. Continue reading...

articles.title: Streeting is desperate to replace Starmer - there's 1 big reason why he'd be a disaster
articles.description: Wes Streeting is bursting for the top job but his time in charge of the NHS proves he's just not up to it.

articles.title: New mobile app to show 'safest way home'
articles.description: The app's creator says its for "everyone who wants to feel more confident when they walk around".

articles.title: Markets await clarity on Iran issue from Trump-Xi talks: Standard Chartered
articles.description: Steve Brice of Standard Chartered said investors watching the talks between President Donald Trump and Chinese President Xi Jinping in Beijing are also awaiting clearer signals on the Iran war. He added that markets are hoping for a quick reopening of the Strait of Hormuz to help relieve disruptions to energy supplies.

articles.title: Early helping behavior shaped by caregiver interactions and motor skills
articles.description: Does your infant put their arm through their sleeve when you get them dressed? As you sort laundry does your toddler pick up the shorts you dropped? These are examples of how infants help by participating in shared activities.

articles.title: ‘Icing’ sports injuries may delay recovery and prolong pain: McGill study
articles.description: Cryotherapy (icing) is often used to reduce pain and swelling, but a preclinical study out of McGill University published in Anesthesiology found that putting ice on a sprained ankle or sore muscle could delay recovery and prolong pain in the long run.

articles.title: New mRNA adjuvant dramatically boosts T-cell vaccine responses in mice
articles.description: MIT engineers have developed a new way to amplify the T-cell response to mRNA vaccines - an advance that could lead to much more powerful cancer vaccines and stronger protection against infectious diseases.

articles.title: Shionogi pill prevents COVID-19 in household exposure study
articles.description: The results mark the first late-stage success for an oral antiviral used to prevent COVID-19 after exposure.

articles.title: Wastewater monitoring may predict influenza outbreaks one week earlier
articles.description: Seasonal influenza can spread rapidly, and timely information on rising cases is essential for public health decisions and healthcare resource planning.

articles.title: New psychedelic-like drugs could treat depression without making you trip
articles.description: UC Davis researchers created brand-new psychedelic-like compounds by shining UV light on amino acid-based molecules. These compounds activated key serotonin receptors tied to brain plasticity and mental health benefits, but surprisingly did not cause hallucination-like behavior in animal tests. Scientists say the discovery could lead to future treatments for depression, PTSD, and addiction without the intense psychedelic experience.

articles.title: Pregnancy not linked to increased myasthenia gravis flare-up risk
articles.description: For female participants with myasthenia gravis, pregnancy is not associated with an increased risk of serious flare-ups of the disease, or new or worsening symptoms, according to a study published May 13, 2026, in Neurology, the medical journal of the American Academy of Neurology.

articles.title: Immune-regulating molecule may offer new treatment approach for IBD patients
articles.description: Mayo Clinic researchers have identified an immune-regulating molecule that may help explain why some patients with inflammatory bowel disease (IBD), including Crohn's disease and ulcerative colitis, do not respond to commonly used therapies.

articles.title: Why experts say PCOS needs a new name
articles.description: A Lancet Health Policy paper reports an international consensus process that selected polyendocrine metabolic ovarian syndrome (PMOS) as the new name for polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS). The change aims to shift the focus away from ovarian cysts and better reflect the condition’s endocrine, metabolic, and ovarian features.

articles.title: Higher body mass index linked to financial problems, loneliness, and stress
articles.description: New research presented at this year's European Congress on Obesity in Istanbul, Turkey (12-15 May) shows that higher body mass index (BMI) is associated with more financial problems, loneliness, and stress.

articles.title: Trump Administration Will Withhold $1.3 Billion in Medicaid Payments to California
articles.description: Vice President JD Vance said the state had not done enough to fight fraud in the public health insurance program.

articles.title: Cerebras reportedly set to price IPO at $185 per share
articles.description: CNBC's Kristina Partsinevelos joins 'Fast Money' with news on Cerebras' IPO.

articles.title: Middle-aged spread can raise the risk of cancer 5-fold, study suggests
articles.description: The warning comes after an analysis of over 600,000 men and women who were weighed repeatedly over four decades found there is no safe age to bulge.

articles.title: MG217 eczema cream recalled nationwide for staph bacteria
articles.description: Consumers who purchased MG217 Multi-symptom Treatment Cream with lot number 1024088 are urged to return the product immediately. People with compromised skin or immune systems face the highest risk.

articles.title: Weight gain as adult increases cancer risk by up to five times, research shows
articles.description: Swedish study of 600,000 patients between 17 and 60 years old suggests there is no safe age to get heavier Gaining weight as an adult increases the risk of cancer by up to five times, according to research involving more than 600,000 patients. Obesity can cause 13 different cancers and is thought to be linked to another eight. But less is known about the impact on cancer risk of the amount of weight put on – and when in life it is gained. Continue reading...

articles.title: Doctors urged to study Mona Lisa for fresh perspective on obesity
articles.description: Learning about art history could help counter stigma around being overweight

articles.title: Cancer warning as rising tide of obesity could finally be levelling off, research shows
articles.description: Global analysis presented at the European Congress on Obesity in Istanbul found rising obesity rates in high-income countries appear to be slowing

articles.title: Third of weight loss from injections is actually muscle and bone - not fat
articles.description: People on weight loss jabs lose muscle and bone mass which they never regain - even if they put the fat back on after they stop taking them, research shows

articles.title: Becoming a mum left me incontinent - we need to talk about birth injuries
articles.description: Geeta Nayar, who has lived with bowel incontinence since her daughter was born, urges others not to suffer in silence.
